pairs.hint: 'Affect' is usually a verb (to influence); 'effect' is usually a noun (a result).
'Then' refers to time; 'than' is used for comparisons.
'Loose' means not tight; 'lose' means to misplace or fail to win.
'Their' is possessive; 'there' is a place; 'they're' means 'they are'.
'Its' is possessive; 'it's' means 'it is' or 'it has'.
'Your' is possessive; 'you're' means 'you are'.
'Accept' means to receive; 'except' means to exclude.
'Compliment' is praise; 'complement' means to complete.
'Principal' means main or a school leader; 'principle' is a rule or belief.
'Stationary' means not moving; 'stationery' is writing paper.
'Advice' is a noun; 'advise' is a verb.
'Breath' is a noun; 'breathe' is a verb.
'Who's' means 'who is'; 'whose' is possessive.
'To' is a preposition; 'too' means also or excessively; 'two' is the number.
'Peace' is tranquility; 'piece' is a part of something.
'Weather' is climate; 'whether' introduces alternatives.
'Desert' is a dry area; 'dessert' is a sweet course.
'Ensure' means to make certain; 'insure' is about insurance; 'assure' means to promise.
'Farther' is physical distance; 'further' is figurative extent.
'Fewer' is for countable items; 'less' is for uncountable quantities.
'Allude' means to reference indirectly; 'elude' means to escape.
'Cite' means to reference; 'site' is a location; 'sight' is vision.
'Discreet' means prudent; 'discrete' means separate.
'Elicit' means to draw out; 'illicit' means illegal.
'Emigrate' is to leave a country; 'immigrate' is to enter a country.
'Eminent' means distinguished; 'imminent' means about to happen.
'Flaunt' means to show off; 'flout' means to disregard.
'Imply' means to suggest; 'infer' means to deduce.
'Lay' requires an object; 'lie' does not.
'Peak' is a summit; 'peek' is a glance; 'pique' means to stimulate.
'Precede' means to come before; 'proceed' means to continue.
'Bare' means uncovered; 'bear' means to carry or the animal.
'Capital' is a city or wealth; 'capitol' is a government building.
'Council' is an assembly; 'counsel' is advice or a lawyer.
'Coarse' means rough; 'course' is a path or class.
'Dual' means double; 'duel' is a fight.
'Envelop' means to surround; 'envelope' is for mail.
'Hoard' is a stockpile; 'horde' is a large group.
'Lead' (present) vs 'led' (past tense of lead).
'Lightening' means becoming lighter; 'lightning' is the weather phenomenon.
'Moral' relates to right/wrong; 'morale' is spirit or confidence.
'Waist' is a body part; 'waste' means to squander.
'All right' is the standard spelling; 'alright' is informal.
'Beside' means next to; 'besides' means in addition to.
'Historic' means significant in history; 'historical' means related to history.
'Averse' means opposed to; 'adverse' means harmful.
'Appraise' means to evaluate; 'apprise' means to inform.
'Chord' is a music term; 'cord' is a string or rope.
'Conscience' is a moral sense; 'conscious' means aware.
'Diffuse' means to spread; 'defuse' means to remove danger.
'Phase' is a stage; 'faze' means to disturb.
'Ware' is merchandise; 'wear' is for clothing; 'where' is a location.
'Wring' means to twist; 'ring' is a circle or bell sound.
'Allusion' is an indirect reference; 'illusion' is a false perception.
'Born' means given birth to; 'borne' means carried.
'Canvas' is fabric; 'canvass' means to solicit.
'Censor' means to suppress; 'censure' means to criticize.
'Compose' means to make up; 'comprise' means to consist of.
'Continual' means recurring; 'continuous' means uninterrupted.
'Credible' means believable; 'credulous' means gullible.
'Definite' means certain; 'definitive' means conclusive.
'Deprecate' means to express disapproval; 'depreciate' means to decrease in value.
'Flounder' means to struggle; 'founder' means to sink or fail.
'Hardy' means robust; 'hearty' means warm and enthusiastic.
'Loath' means reluctant; 'loathe' means to hate.
'Material' is substance; 'materiel' is military equipment.
'Militate' means to work against; 'mitigate' means to lessen.
'Personal' means private; 'personnel' means staff.
'Prescribe' means to recommend; 'proscribe' means to forbid.
'Tortuous' means winding; 'torturous' means painful.
